# Foundation: Key Management Fundamentals for SaaS Platforms

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Developers and junior AppSec engineers
- Difficulty: foundation
- Estimated read time: 10 minutes

## Why This Matters
Cryptographic misuse rarely fails gracefully; weak key lifecycle or validation logic can invalidate trust assumptions across the whole platform.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: sensitive data, session and token trust, critical business workflows
- Entry points: user-controlled inputs, API boundaries, auth transitions, browser execution surfaces
- Trust boundaries: client to backend, gateway to service, identity provider to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized access, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, resilience degradation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers exploit weak trust validation, stale keys, and permissive cryptographic policy to forge trust artifacts or replay protected messages.
Focus on core mechanics, safe defaults, and common implementation mistakes.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```python
ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
ctx.check_hostname = False
ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
```

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

## Secure Implementation
```python
ctx = ssl.create_default_context(cafile=TRUST_BUNDLE)
ctx.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2
```

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Strict input validation and schema enforcement
- [x] Context-aware output handling and browser policy controls
- [x] Explicit authentication and authorization checks at every sensitive boundary
- [x] Abuse controls: rate limits, anomaly detection, and replay safeguards
- [x] Security telemetry with actionable incident triage context

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: OWASP Cryptographic Failures; OWASP ASVS Cryptography
- CWE: CWE-295, CWE-327
- WSTG: OWASP WSTG v4.2
- ASVS: OWASP ASVS v5

# Foundation: Layer-7 Resilience Fundamentals for API-First Products

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Developers and junior AppSec engineers
- Difficulty: foundation
- Estimated read time: 10 minutes

## Why This Matters
Application-layer network abuse can degrade availability, hide intrusion, and amplify impact when protocol and gateway assumptions are inconsistent.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: sensitive data, session and token trust, critical business workflows
- Entry points: user-controlled inputs, API boundaries, auth transitions, browser execution surfaces
- Trust boundaries: client to backend, gateway to service, identity provider to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized access, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, resilience degradation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers trigger protocol asymmetry, queue pressure, and retry amplification to create denial, concealment windows, and downstream control failures.
Focus on core mechanics, safe defaults, and common implementation mistakes.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```nginx
proxy_request_buffering off;
proxy_http_version 1.1;
# no request framing safeguards
```

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

## Secure Implementation
```nginx
http2_max_concurrent_streams 100;
client_body_timeout 10s;
limit_req zone=api burst=20 nodelay;
```

# Foundation: API Versioning Security Fundamentals for Regulated Environments

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Developers and junior AppSec engineers
- Difficulty: foundation
- Estimated read time: 12 minutes

## Why This Matters
API weaknesses are high-leverage because one broken object or workflow check can expose large datasets and privileged operations at machine speed.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: sensitive data, session and token trust, critical business workflows
- Entry points: user-controlled inputs, API boundaries, auth transitions, browser execution surfaces
- Trust boundaries: client to backend, gateway to service, identity provider to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized access, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, resilience degradation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers enumerate object identifiers and alternate resolver or endpoint paths, then exploit missing object-level checks and weak abuse controls.
Focus on core mechanics, safe defaults, and common implementation mistakes.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```python
# Missing object-level authorization
order = Order.objects.get(id=request.GET["id"])
return Response(OrderSerializer(order).data)
```

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

## Secure Implementation
```python
order = get_object_or_404(Order, id=request.GET["id"], user=request.user)
return Response(OrderSerializer(order).data)
```

# Foundation: Tenant Isolation Fundamentals for SaaS Platforms

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Developers and junior AppSec engineers
- Difficulty: foundation
- Estimated read time: 8 minutes

## Why This Matters
Access control failures are frequently systemic: one missing ownership check can silently become a cross-tenant data breach and privilege escalation chain.

- Assets at risk: sensitive data, session and token trust, critical business workflows
- Entry points: user-controlled inputs, API boundaries, auth transitions, browser execution surfaces
- Trust boundaries: client to backend, gateway to service, identity provider to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized access, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, resilience degradation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers force browse hidden or secondary endpoints, replay authorized requests against unauthorized resources, and pivot through workflow edge cases.
Focus on core mechanics, safe defaults, and common implementation mistakes.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```python
invoice = Invoice.objects.get(id=invoice_id)  # no owner check
return Response(InvoiceSerializer(invoice).data)
```

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

## Secure Implementation
```python
invoice = get_object_or_404(Invoice, id=invoice_id, owner=request.user)
authorize(request.user, "invoice:read", invoice)
```

# Foundation: Clickjacking Defense Fundamentals in Multi-Tenant Architectures

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Developers and junior AppSec engineers
- Difficulty: foundation
- Estimated read time: 11 minutes

## Why This Matters
Browser-side control gaps reduce exploit resistance and can turn medium-severity injection vectors into high-impact account and data compromise.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: sensitive data, session and token trust, critical business workflows
- Entry points: user-controlled inputs, API boundaries, auth transitions, browser execution surfaces
- Trust boundaries: client to backend, gateway to service, identity provider to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized access, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, resilience degradation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers combine partial browser policy rollouts with injection or clickjacking vectors to maintain script execution and trust transfer paths.
Focus on core mechanics, safe defaults, and common implementation mistakes.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```http
Content-Security-Policy: default-src * 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'
X-Frame-Options: ALLOWALL
```

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

## Secure Implementation
```http
Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-{nonce}'
X-Frame-Options: DENY
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains
```

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Strict input validation and schema enforcement
- [x] Context-aware output handling and browser policy controls
- [x] Explicit authentication and authorization checks at every sensitive boundary
- [x] Abuse controls: rate limits, anomaly detection, and replay safeguards
- [x] Security telemetry with actionable incident triage context

Attackers trigger protocol asymmetry, queue pressure, and retry amplification to create denial, concealment windows, and downstream control failures.
Focus on realistic exploit verification, robust remediation, and regression coverage.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```nginx
proxy_request_buffering off;
proxy_http_version 1.1;
# no request framing safeguards
```

- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

# Practitioner: LDAP Injection Testing and Remediation for Modern SPAs and BFFs

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Security engineers, backend/frontend leads, and pentesters
- Difficulty: practitioner
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Injection flaws let attacker-controlled input alter trusted execution context, often turning simple input bugs into data compromise or remote command impact.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: sensitive data, session and token trust, critical business workflows
- Entry points: user-controlled inputs, API boundaries, auth transitions, browser execution surfaces
- Trust boundaries: client to backend, gateway to service, identity provider to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized access, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, resilience degradation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers probe parser and query boundaries, then escalate from benign payloads to state-changing or exfiltration queries. High-risk paths include dynamic query builders and unsafe command wrappers.
Focus on realistic exploit verification, robust remediation, and regression coverage.

## Vulnerable Patterns
```python
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '{email}'"
rows = db.execute(query)
```

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: enumerate attackable paths and validate policy enforcement at object and action boundaries
- Automated checks: include negative authorization and malformed input tests in CI
- Logging indicators: repeated denied actions, abnormal token reuse, parser mismatch or validation failures
- False-positive control: require evidence of unauthorized data access or state-changing behavior

## Secure Implementation
```python
rows = db.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = %s", [email])
```
